The public vow was spoken before granite arch: to guard the river, to tell truth when hunger gnawed, to honor the pact as long as breath kept time, always. The binding act followed: every elder pressed their palm into a bowl of living ash and drank the bitter rain, sealing the covenant with blood and salt that burned on the tongue. The ritual was painful, but clear, a memory etched into the future.

A wind rose, bearing a temptation to break the vow: gold that never tarnished, a door that opened to power without toil, a whisper promising ease at the cost of honor. Some dreamed of slipping away, tempted to betray the trust that held the village together. Yet the tale did not end there.

Over years the consequence grew inevitable: drought softened into patience, famine loosened its grip because the river kept returning to those who kept faith; crops learned to listen to the drumbeat of time; strangers learned to trust the Salt-Watchers. The oath, once costly, became the ground beneath feet, a rhythm nobody could gladly abandon.
